topic: A neighbor's tree falls on their property
raw_output:
[story 1]

The wind had been whistling a low, unsettling tune all afternoon, making the leaves on my porch rustle with an alarming, hollow sound. I kept glancing toward the towering oak at the edge of the yard, a deep knot tightening in my stomach with every gust. Every shadow seemed to shift, and the silence between the gusts felt heavy, expectant. My hands, resting on the railing, were slick with a sudden cold sweat. I squeezed my eyes shut for a brief second, trying to steady the rapid thumping beneath my ribs. Then, a sharp crack split the air, followed by a sickening groan that seemed to vibrate through the very ground beneath my feet. I sprang up, my breath catching in my throat, and bolted toward the window, peering out into the darkening expanse where the tree stood.

[story 2]

I couldn't settle down. The sound of the wind through the branches of Mr. Henderson's massive maple had been nagging at me since lunchtime. I kept checking my phone, the screen light illuminating the frantic lines of my own palm. My jaw was clenched so tightly I could feel the muscles straining in my neck. Every time a branch scraped against the siding of my house, I flinched, a tiny, involuntary jerk of my shoulders. I paced the length of my living room three times, my footsteps muffled by the thick carpet, a restless energy buzzing just beneath my skin. When the sound finally came—a monstrous, splintering crash—I froze mid-stride, my entire body locking up, waiting for the inevitable consequence.

[story 3]

The air felt thick, suffocating, pressing down on my chest. I kept replaying the sight of the tree in my mind, the way its silhouette looked against the bruised twilight sky. My focus narrowed until the world consisted only of that single point of potential disaster. I started pacing the small patch of lawn near the fence line, my movements jerky and too quick. My gaze darted from the horizon to the tree, then back again, unable to let my eyes rest on any one place for too long. A faint tremor ran through my hands, and I instinctively brought them up to my mouth, biting down hard on my lower lip until the sharp taste of blood grounded me momentarily. Then, the sound erupted—a violent, tearing noise that stole the breath right out of my lungs.
